This is a low-effort, high-payoff braise: a chuck roast married to chipotle, dark beer, and a square of dark chocolate, then slow-cooked overnight until it falls apart. A finishing reduction concentrates the sauce into something glossy and rich, and the whole dish only gets better after a night in the fridge—so plan on making it a day ahead if you can.
Three shredded beef barbacoa tacos on flour tortillas, topped with cilantro, red onion, and cotija, with lime wedges on the side
3 lbs beef chuck roast, cut into 3-4 inch chunks
Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper
5-6 chipotle peppers in adobo, chopped, plus 2-3 tbsp adobo sauce
1 bottle (12 oz) dark or amber beer (see notes)
1 (10 oz) can mild red enchilada sauce
1 oz dark chocolate (or 1 tsp cocoa powder)
1 tbsp additional adobo sauce, reserved (optional but recommended)

Pat the beef dry and season generously on all sides with kosher salt and black pepper. Heat the oil in a large skillet or Dutch oven over high heat until shimmering. Working in batches to avoid crowding the pan, sear the beef until deeply browned on all sides, about 3-4 minutes per side. Transfer to the slow cooker.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the diced onion to the same pan and cook until softened, about 4-5 minutes. Stir in the garlic, chipotles with their adobo, and tomato paste. Cook for 2 minutes, until the paste darkens to a brick red. Add the cumin, ancho, smoked paprika, and oregano and toast for 30 seconds, just until fragrant.
Pour in the Coca-Cola and beer, scraping up all the browned bits from the bottom of the pan. Let bubble for 1 minute, then pour everything into the slow cooker over the beef.

Add the beef broth, brown sugar, green chiles, enchilada sauce, and cinnamon stick to the slow cooker. Stir to combine. Cover and cook on LOW for 8-9 hours (or HIGH for 5, if pressed for time). The beef is done when it shreds easily with a fork.

Transfer the beef to a bowl. Discard the cinnamon stick. Strain the braising liquid into a saucepan, skim off the surface fat, and simmer over medium-high heat for 10-15 minutes, until reduced by about a third and slightly syrupy—it should coat the back of a spoon. While the liquid reduces, shred the beef with two forks.

Off the heat, stir the dark chocolate into the reduced sauce until melted. Add the lime juice and the reserved tablespoon of adobo sauce. Taste and adjust for salt. Return the shredded beef to the slow cooker, pour the sauce over the top, and stir to combine. Let it rest on the WARM setting for at least 20 minutes so the meat reabsorbs the sauce. Stir in the chopped cilantro just before serving.

This dish improves dramatically overnight. After the slow cook, refrigerate the meat and unreduced liquid separately—the fat solidifies into a clean disc on top of the liquid and lifts off easily the next day, giving a cleaner reduction. Reduce, finish, and combine in the morning; leftovers keep 4-5 days in the fridge and reheat beautifully in the microwave.
This is the single biggest flavor upgrade in the recipe. A wet, unseared piece of meat in a slow cooker is a missed opportunity.
Cooking on LOW yields a silkier, more cohesive texture as collagen breaks down at a gentler temperature. Use HIGH only when time is tight.

5-6 chipotles plus 2-3 tablespoons of adobo sauce is a calibrated baseline—warm and smoky, but broadly friendly to people who don't seek out heat. Spice-lovers can push to 7-8 chipotles or add cayenne. The extra tablespoon of adobo stirred in during the finish is where a lot of the noticeable heat lives, since fresh-added chile reads hotter than chile that's been braised for nine hours.

Any dark or amber beer works: Modelo Negra, Dos Equis Ambar, Bohemia, brown ales, porters, and stouts. Avoid IPAs and light lagers—hoppy bitterness turns aggressive when reduced, and light lagers add nothing. To skip beer entirely, substitute ½ cup brewed coffee plus ½ cup additional beef broth.
A five-minute blender shake for using up ripe bananas and hitting a protein number without tasting like a supplement. Frozen fruit keeps it thick without watering it down the way ice does.
1 scoop vanilla or chocolate ice cream
1 scoop chocolate or vanilla protein powder
Combine the ice cream, frozen fruit, banana, protein powder, and milk in a blender. Blend on high until smooth.
Add water a splash at a time until the shake reaches your preferred consistency, blending briefly between additions. Pour and serve immediately.
The riper (more speckled) the banana, the sweeter the shake—freeze overripe bananas in chunks ahead of time so you always have one on hand.

Bacon, cheddar, and softly scrambled eggs stacked between griddled mini bagels, brushed with an everything-bagel butter and baked until the cheese pulls. It's a hand-held take on the diner breakfast sandwich, built to assemble ahead for a crowd and bake off in one pan.

Preheat the oven to 350°F and grease a 9x13-inch baking dish. Cook the bacon in a large skillet over medium heat, turning occasionally, until crisp, 8-10 minutes. Transfer to a paper-towel-lined plate and pour off all but 1 tablespoon of fat.

Whisk the eggs and milk together until light and frothy. Pour into the same skillet and cook over medium-low heat, stirring often, until just barely set, 2-3 minutes. Season with salt and pepper and stir in the chives, then remove from the heat immediately—the eggs will keep cooking in the residual heat.
Spread the cream cheese over the bottom halves of the bagels and arrange side by side in the prepared dish. Layer with the scrambled eggs, crumbled bacon, and cheddar, then close with the top halves.
Brush the tops with melted butter and sprinkle with the dried onion, garlic, poppy seeds, and sesame seeds. Bake until the bagels are toasted and the cheese has melted, about 10 minutes. Serve warm.
Assemble through the buttering step a day ahead, cover, and refrigerate. Add a few extra minutes to the bake time if going in cold.
Sharp cheddar is classic here, but pepper jack or gruyère both melt beautifully if you want to change the flavor profile.

A weeknight staple built around a from-scratch alfredo: seared chicken breast, a garlic-and-Parmesan cream sauce loosened with starchy pasta water, and just enough white wine to keep it from tasting heavy. On the table in well under an hour.
Kosher salt, for the pasta water
½ cup dry white wine (or chicken broth)
1 cup finely grated Parmesan, plus more for serving
½ cup reserved pasta water, as needed
Bring a large pot of heavily salted water to a boil and cook the penne until just al dente, according to package directions. Reserve ½ cup pasta water, then drain.

Heat the olive oil in a large sauté pan over medium heat. Season the chicken on both sides with oregano, garlic powder, paprika, salt, and pepper. Sear until deeply golden on each side and cooked through, 4-6 minutes per side. Transfer to a plate to rest.

Melt the butter in the same pan over medium-low heat. Add the garlic, oregano, and a pinch each of salt and pepper, and cook, stirring frequently, for 1 minute until fragrant. Stir in the flour and cook for another minute, stirring constantly.

Pour in the white wine, scraping up any browned bits from the bottom of the pan, and let it bubble for 1 minute. Whisk in the heavy cream and simmer over medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, until smooth and slightly thickened, a few minutes.
Stir in the Parmesan until melted and the sauce reaches a glossy, coating consistency. Season to taste with salt and pepper.

Slice the rested chicken into strips. Add the pasta to the sauce and toss to coat, thinning with a splash of reserved pasta water as needed. Fold in the chicken and toss until everything is warmed through. Garnish with extra Parmesan and parsley.
Don't skip this—it's your first and best chance to season the noodles themselves, and it carries through into the sauce once the pasta water goes in.

Cutting each breast in half lengthwise gives you two thinner cutlets that cook through fully during the 4-6 minute sear. If a piece is still pink at the center, finish it in a 350°F oven while you build the sauce.
The sauce thickens as it cools
Loosen leftovers with a splash of cream, milk, or broth when reheating.

A pantry-speed pasta built on browned Italian sausage and jarred sauce, dressed up with garlic, shallot, and a handful of optional finishers. Equally at home as a 25-minute weeknight dinner or, with the cream and fresh herbs added, something worth serving to guests.
1 lb Italian sausage (sweet or spicy), casings removed
24 oz jarred or canned pasta sauce
1 tsp kosher salt, plus more to taste
½ cup heavy cream or 4 tbsp butter
3 tbsp fresh basil or parsley, chopped

Heat a large skillet over medium-high heat. Add the sausage and cook, breaking it into chunks as it browns, until deeply caramelized and cooked through, 6-8 minutes. Don't rush this step—the browning is most of the flavor. Drain excess fat, leaving about 1 tablespoon in the pan.
Add the shallots and cook for 4 minutes, until softened. Stir in the garlic and red pepper flakes, if using, and cook for 1 minute more, until fragrant.

Pour in the pasta sauce and stir to combine with the sausage and aromatics. Simmer on low for 9-10 minutes to let the flavors come together. Season to taste with salt and pepper, then stir in the cream or butter, if using, for extra richness.
While the sauce simmers, bring a large pot of salted water to a boil. Cook the pasta until al dente according to package directions. Reserve 1 cup of pasta water before draining.
Add the drained pasta to the sauce and toss gently to coat, loosening with reserved pasta water as needed. Top with Parmesan and fresh herbs, if using, and a squeeze of lemon juice for brightness.
Cook the sausage past the point of "done"—all the way to deeply caramelized. That's where the depth of flavor comes from, and jarred sauce needs the help.
It's the easiest way to loosen and emulsify the sauce without watering down the flavor—always reserve a cup before draining.
Fresh basil or parsley added during cooking turns dull and bitter; stir them in right before serving to keep them bright.
